mission.title: Install and Configure Tikv on Kubernetes
mission.description: TiKV is an open-source, distributed, and transactional key-value database that provides ACID-compliant transactional APIs. Installing TiKV allows you to leverage its powerful features for managing large-scale data with high consistency and horizontal scalability.

steps.title: Add the TiKV Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the TiKV Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add tikv https://charts.pingcap.org
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install TiKV using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install TiKV using the Helm chart, specifying the desired namespace.
```bash
helm install tikv tikv/tikv --namespace tikv --create-namespace --version v8.5.5
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the TiKV pods are running successfully in the specified namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace tikv
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the TiKV deployment. Edit the values file or use the following command:
```bash
helm upgrade tikv tikv/tikv --namespace tikv --set resources.limits.cpu=2000m --set resources.limits.memory=4Gi
```

steps.title: Set up monitoring (optional)
steps.description:
For better observability, you can enable monitoring by integrating TiKV with Prometheus. Update your Helm installation:
```bash
helm upgrade tikv tikv/tikv --namespace tikv --set monitoring.enabled=true
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of TiKV will show the TiKV pods running in the 'tikv' namespace. You can also verify the resource limits and monitoring settings applied to the deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add tikv https://charts.pingcap.org
helm repo update
helm install tikv tikv/tikv --namespace tikv --create-namespace --version v8.5.5
helm upgrade tikv tikv/tikv --namespace tikv --set resources.limits.cpu=2000m --set resources.limits.memory=4Gi
helm upgrade tikv tikv/tikv --namespace tikv --set monitoring.enabled=true

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the TiKV deployment, use the following command to uninstall the Helm release from the specified namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall tikv --namespace tikv
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After uninstalling the Helm release, you should clean up any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd tikvclusters.pingcap.com
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace tikv
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, remove the namespace used for TiKV and verify that it has been deleted:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace tikv
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, it's important to back up your current state. You can create a backup of your existing TiKV data by exporting it or taking snapshots, depending on your setup.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the TiKV release to the latest version. Run the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade tikv tikv/tikv --namespace tikv --version v8.5.6
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, verify that the TiKV pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace tikv
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your TiKV pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace tikv
```
Look for any configuration issues or resource constraints that may be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set for the TiKV deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment tikv --namespace tikv
```
Adjust the resource limits in your Helm upgrade command if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Failed to connect to TiKV
troubleshooting.description:
If your application fails to connect to TiKV, ensure that the service is running and accessible:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace tikv
```
Check for any network policies or firewall rules that might be blocking access.

troubleshooting.title: Persistent volume issues
troubleshooting.description:
If there are issues with persistent volumes, check the status of the PVCs:
```bash
kubectl get pvc --namespace tikv
```
Ensure that the PVCs are bound and that the underlying storage is available.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
